Name: Pattern Recognition - PE
Text: First lets understand the requirements 1 Identify cases with the sequence Approve Purchase Requisition Create Order Confirm Order in direct succession no other events in between 2 Among those cases filter further to only those where the time between Create Order and Confirm Order is more than 5 days 3 Exclude these identified cases from the final result 4 Return all events from the remaining cases

Name: Hypothesis Generation - PE
Text: Approach We need to find cases that have the specific pattern with the timing condition Then exclude those case_ids from the main event log

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: First identify the sequences Since we need direct succession we can use window functions to look at the next events For each event we can look at the next event and the event after that using LEAD

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Pattern matching logic For a case to match there must exist three consecutive events by timestamp where Event 1 activity Approve Purchase Requisition Event 2 activity Create Order next event Event 3 activity Confirm Order event after next And the time between Event 2 and Event 3 more than 5 days

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: Implementation details Use LEAD activity 1 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp to get next activity Use LEAD activity 2 OVER PARTITION BY case_id ORDER BY timestamp to get the activity after next Use LEAD timestamp 1 OVER and LEAD timestamp 2 OVER to get timestamps Identify rows where activity Approve Purchase Requisition LEAD activity 1 Create Order LEAD activity 2 Confirm Order AND next next timestamp minus next timestamp more than interval 5 days

Name: Validation - PE
Text: However we need to be careful The above identifies the starting row of the sequence But we need to mark the entire case as to be excluded

Name: Deductive Reasoning - PE
Text: So the plan is Create a CTE or subquery that identifies all case_id values that have this pattern with the timing condition Then select from event_log where case_id is not in that set

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Wait is this correct for direct succession The prompt says no other event from the same case in between them My query checks that row N is Approve row N plus 1 is Create row N plus 2 is Confirm This ensures no other events are between them because they are consecutive rows in the ordered partition

Name: Abductive Reasoning - IND
Text: However we should consider if there can be duplicate timestamps The problem says direct succession which usually implies ordering by timestamp but if timestamps are equal the order might be ambiguous However standard SQL window functions with ORDER BY timestamp would assign an arbitrary order to ties But usually in event logs we assume timestamp is unique or we can add a row number or unique ID to break ties But the prompt does not mention an additional ordering column I will assume timestamp ordering is sufficient

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Let me double check the timing condition time elapsed between the Create Order event and the Confirm Order event of that sequence is more than 5 days In my query next_timestamp corresponds to Create Order next_next_timestamp corresponds to Confirm Order So the difference is next_next_timestamp minus next_timestamp which represents the time between Create and Confirm This looks correct

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - IND
Text: Alternative approach using self joins We could join the table with itself multiple times but that is less efficient and more verbose Window functions are better

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Another edge case What if a case has multiple such sequences and only one of them meets the criteria The case should still be excluded My query uses DISTINCT case_id so even if multiple rows in a case match the pattern we only list the case_id once in the exclusion list This is correct

Name: Validation - PE
Text: What if the sequence appears but not with the timing condition Then the case should not be excluded My query correctly filters for the timing condition in the WHERE clause of the subquery

Name: Validation - IND
Text: DuckDB syntax INTERVAL 5 days is standard SQL and works in DuckDB Window functions LEAD OVER work in DuckDB DISTINCT in CTE is fine NOT IN is fine though NOT EXISTS is sometimes preferred for performance with NULLs

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually NOT IN can have issues if the subquery returns NULL But case_id is an identifier and should not be NULL To be safe we could use NOT EXISTS

Name: Validation - IND
Text: This avoids the CTE and the potential NULL issue with NOT IN It might be less efficient if the window function is calculated for every check

Name: Backtracking - PE
Text: Actually the subquery in NOT EXISTS references e case_id so the window function would need to be recalculated for each case Explicitly materializing the bad cases in a CTE is safer and clearer So I will stick with the CTE approach but maybe switch to LEFT JOIN IS NULL

Name: Heuristic Reasoning - PE
Text: LEFT JOIN IS NULL is the standard anti join pattern very efficient

Name: Validation - PE
Text: This looks good It is clear uses standard SQL and should work in DuckDB

Name: Validation - PE
Text: Final check on requirements 1 Sequence in direct succession Yes checked by LEAD offsets 1 and 2 2 Time between Create and Confirm more than 5 days Yes checked by next next timestamp minus next timestamp more than interval 5 days 3 Exclude those cases Yes using LEFT JOIN IS NULL anti join 4 Return all events from other cases Yes selecting from event_log and filtering

Name: Validation - PE
Text: What if the case has overlapping sequences or interruptions The LEAD function picks only truly consecutive events and a case is excluded if there exists at least one row where the full condition sequence plus timing is met
